turns.content: how do you prepare for an annual performance review when you're lowkey terrified

First — the terror is normal. Almost everyone feels it regardless of how well they've actually done. When is your review, and what kind of workplace is it? That'll help me give you more specific advice.

April 8th. I'm at PwC, in advisory. deals and transactions team. and this is my first review since I moved to this team so there's extra pressure because I feel like I need to prove the move was worth it

Okay, so a Big 4 annual review with the added layer of having recently changed teams — that's a specific kind of pressure. The good news is that internal moves at PwC are generally seen positively by reviewers because it shows initiative and adaptability. What's your gut feeling about how the year went?

honestly I think I've done well? like I've been on some big deals and got positive feedback from the managers I've worked with. but PwC reviews are weird because you can do great work and still get a middling rating if the calibration goes against you

You're right that Big 4 calibration sessions can be unpredictable — they're essentially ranking you against your peer group, so individual performance isn't the only factor. But going in with documented evidence of your contributions makes a massive difference. Have you started pulling together your self-assessment yet?

not properly tbh. I've been so busy on current deals that I haven't had time to sit down and actually write it. I know I need to but every time I think about it I just stare at the blank form and freeze

The blank form paralysis is real. Let's break it down so it feels less overwhelming. Start with this: list every deal or engagement you worked on since you joined advisory. Don't write paragraphs yet, just list them. For each one, write one line about what you specifically did — not what the team did, what YOU did. Can you think of three deals off the top of your head?

yeah so there was the healthcare acquisition where I led the financial due diligence workstream for the first time. then there was a carve-out for a manufacturing client where I built the standalone cost model. and right now I'm on a tech deal doing the quality of earnings analysis

Those are genuinely strong examples — leading a diligence workstream, building a standalone cost model, and QoE work. Each of those shows a different capability. For your self-assessment, frame each one using a simple structure: what was the situation, what did you do specifically, and what was the outcome or impact. For example, the healthcare one — did the deal close? Did your diligence uncover anything significant? Did the client or partner give you any specific feedback?

the healthcare one closed yeah and the partner actually emailed me separately to say my analysis on the working capital adjustments was really thorough. I should dig that email out actually. and the carve-out, the client specifically asked for me on follow-up work which is apparently a big deal according to my manager

Both of those are gold for a review. A partner complimenting your work in writing and a client requesting you by name — those are exactly the kind of proof points that carry weight in calibration. Definitely find that email. In Big 4, the people who get the best ratings aren't always the ones who did the best work — they're the ones who documented and articulated their impact most effectively. Your reviewer is going to have to argue for your rating in a room full of other partners, so you need to give them ammunition.

that's such a good point. I need to make it easy for them to fight for me basically. okay what about areas for development? they always ask that and I never know what to say. like I don't want to give them a reason to mark me down but I also can't say 'nothing I'm perfect' loool

The development section is a game within a game. You want to pick something that's genuine enough to show self-awareness but that's either already improving or is a stretch goal rather than a core weakness. Good options for someone at your level in advisory: wanting to develop more client-facing presentation skills, building deeper sector expertise in a specific industry, or getting more exposure to deal origination rather than just execution. Those are all "I want to do more senior things" disguised as development areas.

ohh that's clever. the client presentation one is actually true tbh. I'm comfortable doing the analysis but when it comes to presenting findings to the client's board I still get nervous. I could frame it as wanting more of those opportunities

Perfect — that's honest, relatable, and positions you as someone who wants to grow into more senior responsibilities. You could even reference a specific moment: "On the healthcare deal I delivered the working capital analysis but I'd like more opportunities to present findings directly to client leadership teams." That shows self-awareness and ambition in the same sentence.

okay I actually feel so much better about this now. I'm going to spend this weekend going through my deals and pulling together the evidence. April 8 is a month away so I've got time to do it properly. thank you for talking me through it I was genuinely spiralling

A month is plenty of time. Block out two hours this weekend just for the deal inventory — list them all out with your specific contributions and any feedback you received. The actual writing of the self-assessment will flow much faster once you have the raw material. And find that partner email. You've had a strong year by the sound of it — the review is just about making sure the right people know it. You've got this.
